title: Posters – Abstracts
abstract: Introduction/objectives. Falls are common among the elderly and represent a major public health issue due to their significant social and economic impact and serious health consequences.The objective of this study was to investigate the associations between insomnia, comorbidities, multisite musculoskeletal pain, physical activity, and fall risk in older adults.Materials and methods.This retrospective crosssectional study included residents of elderly care homes in Timioara, Romania.Participants aged 65 years and older were divided into two groups: those without fractures (Group I) and those with a history of fractures (Group II).Sleep quality was assessed using a single item from the Assessment of Quality of Life (AQoL) questionnaire on a 4-point scale.Fall risk was evaluated using the Falls Risk Assessment Tool (FRAT).Results/Conclusions.A total of 140 participants were included, with a mean age of 78.4 2.4 years (range: 65-98 years), of whom 55 were male (39%).Compared to participants without fractures, those with a history of fractures had significantly more comorbidities, higher fall risk scores, and more severe sleep disturbances.Univariate logistic regression revealed significant associations between fracture occurrence and comorbidities, fall risk, and sleep disturbances (p < 0.0001).Multivariate regression identified four independent predictors of fractures: number of comorbidities (p = 0.03), fall risk score (p = 0.006), and sleep disturbances of type 3 (p = 0.003) and type 4 (p = 0.001).A FRAT score greater than 14 and more than two comorbidities were significantly associated with fracture risk.Strong positive correlations were also observed between the severity of sleep disturbances, fall risk scores, and fracture incidence.
